results.possibleIdentity: Hans H. Noe (1928–2025) was a distinguished architect and sculptor based in New York. He is best known for his collaborative relationship with the influential sculptor Tony Smith, whom he assisted with complex numerical calculations and technical drafting for large-scale geometric sculptures. This collaboration was particularly significant in projects such as Smith's "Bat Cave" (1971), which was featured in the landmark "Art and Technology" exhibition at the Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA). Noe's work as an architect bridged the gap between structural engineering and artistic form, a role closely aligned with the mission of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Born in Czernowitz, Austro-Hungary, Noe later moved to the United States, where he designed several buildings, including a home for his family in Garrison, NY, and a separate studio section for the Abstract Expressionist painter Elaine de Kooning. He was a central figure in the SoHo art community and purchased the building housing the historic Fanelli’s Cafe in 1982.

results.possibleIdentity: Michael Carson (1947–2005) was a prominent Australian television director and producer who was active in the early 1970s and throughout the following decades. He began his career as a cadet journalist with the ABC in 1966 before moving into the drama division. He is well-regarded for his work on landmark Australian television series such as "Certain Women" (1973) and "Rush" (1974), and he later produced and directed numerous acclaimed productions including "Heartbreak High," "Police Rescue," and "Blue Murder." His career was marked by a commitment to realistic visual styles and narrative depth in television and film.

Michael Carson (often referred to as Mike Carson) was a key staff member and administrator for Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), the non-profit organization co-founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg to foster collaborations between artists and engineers. Carson served as the Business Manager of E.A.T. during its most active period in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was involved in the management and technical coordination of major projects, including the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan, and the organization's technical services which paired artists with scientists and engineers from institutions like Bell Laboratories.

Michael Carson (Mike Carson) is a scientist and computer graphics pioneer known for his foundational work in macromolecular visualization. While primarily a scientist, his creation of the "Ribbons" software (first described in the 1980s but rooted in research starting in the 1970s) is a significant intersection of art and technology, providing aesthetically driven computer-generated models of protein structures. He was a graduate student at the University of Texas at Austin in the early 1970s, where he began developing these schematic representations which are widely cited in both scientific and digital art contexts for their visual innovation.
